title: Copy and Adapt an Existing Translation Table
breadcrumbs: Copy and Adapt an Existing Translation Table
text: Copy and Adapt an Existing Translation Table It is easier to adapt an existing table than to create a new one from scratch. An existing definition can match your particular device quite closely, if not exactly. Contact the device's distributor for a list of the control characters the device uses. To copy and adapt an existing translation table: Import the Uniface table definitions. Start the Uniface IDE with the /imp command line switch, specifying the applicable XML file: /misc/libdvc.usys.xml for device translation tables or /misc/libktt.usys.xml for keyboard translation tables. /imp d:/uniface/misc/libdvc.usys.xml Copy These files contains the USYS translation table libraries. Click More Editors and choose Device Tables or Keyboard Tables (depending on the type of translation table). If you have not yet created your own translation table library, create one with at least one table and press OK . In the editor, choose File > Open/New , select the USYS library and translation table you want to modify, and click OK . The Translation Table editor is opened. For example: Duplicate the translation table: Choose File > Duplicate . Navigate to and select the library in which to create the new table. Note: Do not define the table in the USYS default library. It contains the standard definitions delivered with Uniface. Tables defined in this library may be overwritten when a new release of Uniface is installed. Provide a name that corresponds to the output device or keyboard. In the Definition , edit the contents of the translation table using the appropriate syntax and mnemonics. For more information, see Device Translation Table Syntax and Keyboard Translation Table Syntax . Choose File > Compile to compile the table. Move the compiled definitions to a deployment archive using the Resources Manager. Related Links Translation Tables To mediate between itself and the external environment, Uniface uses translation tables. Translation tables provide input and output mappings between platform-specific device codes and Uniface functions, and between external and internal character sets. Uniface provides translation tables for the platforms it supports, but you can customize these or use them to create your own tables. Guidelines for Defining Keyboard Translation Tables When defining or adapting keyboard translation tables, there are some general guidelines you should follow. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
